prompt: Edo-period Japanese woodblock print in the style of Utagawa Hiroshige's landscape series, circa 1830–1850. A mountain landscape seen from a high vantage point. The mountain has multiple descending ridgelines and valleys, some shallow and some deep. A lone traveler in traditional Japanese travelling clothes walks a winding path that descends toward the lowest and deepest valley in the composition. Other paths branch off and terminate in false, shallower valleys higher up the mountain. The composition uses flat unmodulated color zones — muted indigo for distant peaks, warm ochre for middle-ground slopes, pale celadon green for the valley floor — separated by bold black keyblock outlines. Sky rendered in horizontal bokashi gradation bands, pale at horizon to deeper blue above. No photographic shading, no cast shadows, no volumetric lighting. Spatial depth conveyed by vertical position on picture plane only. Fine parallel hatching lines on rock faces only where Hiroshige used them for texture. Authentic woodblock print aesthetic including slight paper texture, registration marks visible at edges. No text, no cartouches, no seal marks within image area. Negative prompt: photorealistic, 3D, shadows, gradients on terrain, perspective vanishing point, Western painting style, glowing, digital, anime, modern, labels, text overlays, diagrams, graphs
tradition_name: Edo-period Japanese woodblock print (ukiyo-e, meisho-e landscape sub-genre)
tradition_approach: Hiroshige's flat color zones separated by keyblock outlines, bokashi sky gradation, vertical-plane depth, figure-subordinate-to-landscape scale — terrain's distinct elevation bands map structurally to a loss surface.
